event: According to tradition, Mount Titano and the surrounding area were inhabited by Etruscan and later Roman communities in antiquity.
Saint Marinus, a Christian stonemason from the island of Rab in Dalmatia, is traditionally born around this period.
Marinus founds a monastic community on Mount Titano to escape persecution, an event traditionally regarded as the founding of San Marino.
Saint Marinus dies, and his community of followers continues to live in self-governing independence on Mount Titano.
The community on Mount Titano survives the upheavals of the fall of the Western Roman Empire and Ostrogothic and Byzantine rule in Italy.
The Placito Feretrano, a document recording a dispute, provides early written evidence of San Marino's autonomous existence and landholdings.
San Marino is recorded as governed by its own consuls, reflecting an established communal self-government.
San Marino emerges as a self-governing commune typical of medieval Italian city-states, ruled by elected officials.
The office of the Captains Regent (Capitani Reggenti) is established as joint heads of state, a system still in use today.
San Marino adopts early communal statutes codifying its laws and governance.
The Statutes of San Marino are further developed, strengthening the republic's legal and constitutional framework.
The nearby community of Chiesanuova joins San Marino, an early expansion of the small republic.
San Marino allies with Pope Pius II against the Malatesta lords of Rimini and gains the castles of Faetano, Fiorentino, Montegiardino, and Serravalle, fixing its modern borders.
Cesare Borgia briefly occupies San Marino during his campaigns in central Italy, but the republic soon regains its freedom.
San Marino adopts a written constitution, the Leges Statutae Republicae Sancti Marini, formalizing its republican institutions.
The Papacy formally recognizes San Marino's independence as the Duchy of Urbino is absorbed into the Papal States.
Cardinal Giulio Alberoni occupies San Marino by military force in an attempt to annex it to the Papal States.
Pope Clement XII recognizes San Marino's rights and restores its independence after the people appeal against Alberoni's occupation.
Napoleon Bonaparte offers to expand San Marino's territory and friendship, which the republic politely declines to preserve its modest independence.
The Congress of Vienna recognizes San Marino's independence, allowing the republic to survive the reordering of post-Napoleonic Europe.
San Marino grants refuge to Italian revolutionary Giuseppe Garibaldi and his followers fleeing Austrian and papal forces.
San Marino signs a treaty of friendship and customs union with the newly unified Kingdom of Italy, periodically renewed thereafter.
Abraham Lincoln accepts honorary citizenship of San Marino, praising the republic as proof that government founded on republican principles can endure.
The new Government Palace (Palazzo Pubblico) is inaugurated as the seat of the republic's institutions.
A popular reform replaces the oligarchic Grand and General Council with a council elected by the citizenry, broadening political participation.
A Sammarinese Fascist Party comes to power, aligning the small republic with Mussolini's regime in Italy.
Women begin to gain limited legal rights, though political enfranchisement remains decades away.
The Fascist government falls and San Marino declares its neutrality during the Second World War.
San Marino is mistakenly bombed by Allied aircraft, killing dozens, and is briefly occupied as the front passes through during the Italian Campaign.
San Marino sheltered around 100,000 refugees during the war despite its tiny size and population.
A coalition of Communists and Socialists wins power, making San Marino home to one of the world's first democratically elected Communist-led governments.
The Rovereta crisis ends Communist-Socialist rule when a rival government is installed with Italian support and a bloodless standoff.
Women are denied the vote in elections, prompting a long campaign for female suffrage in the republic.
Women win the right to vote in San Marino, expanding democratic participation in the republic.
Women gain the right to hold public office, completing their political enfranchisement in San Marino.
A new Declaration of the Rights of Citizens establishes a modern bill of fundamental rights and constitutional principles.
Maria Lea Pedini-Angelini becomes one of the first women to serve as Captain Regent, a milestone for gender equality in government.
San Marino joins the Council of Europe, deepening its integration into European institutions.
San Marino establishes a customs union and cooperation agreement with the European Economic Community.
San Marino is admitted to the United Nations as a full member state on March 2.
San Marino adopts the euro as its currency under a monetary agreement with the European Union, despite not being an EU member.
The historic centre of San Marino and Mount Titano are inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site.
Voters reject a referendum on whether to apply for European Union membership amid debate over the republic's economic future.
San Marino joins the wider single euro payments area and continues reforms to its financial and banking sector after global scrutiny.
San Marino legalizes voluntary termination of pregnancy following a referendum, modernizing its social legislation.
Voters overwhelmingly approve legalizing abortion in the first months of pregnancy, ending one of Europe's strictest bans.
San Marino makes its debut at the Tokyo Olympics medal table, becoming the smallest nation ever to win an Olympic medal.
San Marino marks continued negotiations toward an association agreement with the European Union to strengthen ties with the single market.
